evals.prompt: I need to call three independent async TypeScript functions. Should I await them sequentially?
evals.expected_output: No. Use Promise.all for independent concurrent calls: const [a, b, c] = await Promise.all([fnA(), fnB(), fnC()]). Awaiting sequentially is 3x slower when operations don't depend on each other. Use Promise.allSettled when you need all results even if some fail.

evals.prompt: Where should I put try/catch blocks in my TypeScript service?
evals.expected_output: Catch only at system boundaries — HTTP handlers, CLI entry points, event consumers — not in every function. Inner functions should throw and let errors propagate. Use catch (e: unknown) and narrow the type before accessing properties. Avoid catch (e: any). Never swallow errors silently.

evals.prompt: How should I structure dependency injection in a TypeScript service without a DI framework?
evals.expected_output: Use constructor injection. Define interfaces for dependencies, pass them in the constructor, store as private readonly fields. This makes dependencies explicit and easy to mock in tests. Avoid module-level singletons and global state. Use export default sparingly — prefer named exports for better tree-shaking and refactoring.
